expanded_description: Fundamental trigonometric identities are the building blocks of trigonometry—equations that are true for all angles where the functions are defined. These identities include reciprocal identities (csc = 1/sin, etc.), quotient identities (tan = sin/cos, cot = cos/sin), Pythagorean identities (sin²θ + cos²θ = 1, etc.), even/odd identities, and cofunction identities. Mastering these identities is essential for simplifying trigonometric expressions, solving trigonometric equations, verifying other identities, and preparing for calculus (where identities are used constantly in derivatives and integrals). The Pythagorean identities come from the unit circle equation x² + y² = 1, connecting geometry to algebra. The quotient identities connect tangent and cotangent to sine and cosine. The even/odd identities tell us which functions are symmetric (even) and which are antisymmetric (odd). The cofunction identities relate complementary angles. In Pre-calculus, mastering these identities builds algebraic fluency with trig functions, prepares students for solving trig equations, and prepares for calculus (identities used in differentiation and integration). Understanding where identities come from (unit circle, right triangles) builds deeper understanding than memorization. This section teaches fundamental identities—reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, even/odd, cofunction—and how to use them for simplification and verification. Mastery of identities is essential for all advanced trigonometry.

core_concept.principle:
Fundamental Trig Identities:

1. Reciprocal Identities:
sin θ = 1/csc θ, csc θ = 1/sin θ
cos θ = 1/sec θ, sec θ = 1/cos θ
tan θ = 1/cot θ, cot θ = 1/tan θ

2. Quotient Identities:
tan θ = sin θ/cos θ
cot θ = cos θ/sin θ

3. Pythagorean Identities:
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 (fundamental)
1 + tan²θ = sec²θ (divide by cos²)
1 + cot²θ = csc²θ (divide by sin²)

4. Even/Odd Identities:
sin(-θ) = -sin θ (odd)
cos(-θ) = cos θ (even)
tan(-θ) = -tan θ (odd)
csc(-θ) = -csc θ (odd)
sec(-θ) = sec θ (even)
cot(-θ) = -cot θ (odd)

5. Cofunction Identities:
sin(90° - θ) = cos θ
cos(90° - θ) = sin θ
tan(90° - θ) = cot θ
(and reciprocals)

Key Uses:
- Simplify trig expressions
- Verify/prove other identities
- Solve trig equations
- Prepare for calculus
core_concept.explanation: Think of trig identities as 'trig algebra'—rules for manipulating trig expressions just like algebraic rules for manipulating algebraic expressions. The Pythagorean identities come from the unit circle (x² + y² = 1 becomes cos²θ + sin²θ = 1). The quotient identities tell you tan is sin/cos (opposite/adjacent = (opposite/hypotenuse)/(adjacent/hypotenuse)). The even/odd identities tell you which functions are symmetric about y-axis (even: cos, sec) and which are symmetric about origin (odd: sin, tan, csc, cot). The cofunction identities relate complementary angles (add to 90°). These identities are tools—you use them to simplify expressions (make them simpler), verify identities (prove two expressions are equal), and solve equations (find angles that satisfy equation). In calculus, you'll use these identities constantly—for derivatives, integrals, limits. Understanding where identities come from (unit circle, right triangles) is better than memorization—you can derive them if you forget. This section teaches fundamental identities—what they are, where they come from, and how to use them.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.1: Visual summary of fundamental trigonometric identities: reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, even/odd, and cofunction identities with relationships shown.

sections.introduction: Reciprocal identities relate each trig function to its reciprocal (sin ↔ csc, cos ↔ sec, tan ↔ cot). Quotient identities express tangent and cotangent in terms of sine and cosine. These are the simplest identities—essential for simplifying expressions and understanding relationships between the six trig functions.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to reciprocal and quotient identities
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.9.2: Introducing reciprocal identities (sin ↔ csc, cos ↔ sec, tan ↔ cot) and quotient identities (tan = sin/cos, cot = cos/sin).

key_concept.definition:
Reciprocal Identities:

sin θ = 1/csc θ
csc θ = 1/sin θ

cos θ = 1/sec θ
sec θ = 1/cos θ

tan θ = 1/cot θ
cot θ = 1/tan θ

Quotient Identities:

tan θ = sin θ/cos θ
(from right triangle: opp/adj = (opp/hyp)/(adj/hyp))

cot θ = cos θ/sin θ
(from right triangle: adj/opp = (adj/hyp)/(opp/hyp))

When Undefined:
- csc, cot undefined when sin = 0
- sec, tan undefined when cos = 0
- cot, tan undefined when denominator = 0

Memory Tips:
- Reciprocal pairs: sin-csc, cos-sec, tan-cot
- Quotient: tan = sin/cos (alphabetical: s before c)
- cot = cos/sin (reverse alphabetical)
key_concept.context: Reciprocal and quotient identities are fundamental—they connect all six trig functions. In calculus, you'll use these to find derivatives and integrals of all six functions. In simplifying expressions, these identities let you rewrite expressions in terms of sin and cos (often simpler). In solving equations, these identities let you convert between functions. Understanding these identities builds trigonometric fluency—you can work with any trig function confidently. The quotient identities come from right triangle definitions—tan = opp/adj, and if you divide sin/cos you get (opp/hyp)/(adj/hyp) = opp/adj = tan. This connection shows why the identities are true. This section teaches reciprocal and quotient identities—relationships between all six trig functions.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.3: Diagram showing reciprocal identity pairs and quotient identity relationships.

worked_examples.title: Using Reciprocal Identities to Simplify

image.caption: Figure 7.9.4: Using reciprocal identities to simplify trigonometric expressions.

step_by_step.calculation: sin θ · csc θ = sin θ · (1/sin θ)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Reciprocal identity: sin · csc = 1; undefined where sin = 0.

worked_examples.title: Using Quotient Identities to Simplify

image.caption: Figure 7.9.5: Using quotient identities to simplify trigonometric expressions.

step_by_step.calculation: tan θ · cos θ = (sin θ/cos θ) · cos θ
tan θ · cos θ = sin θ (cos θ ≠ 0)

worked_examples.concept_applied: Quotient identity: tan = sin/cos; use to simplify expressions with tan.

worked_examples.title: Combining Reciprocal and Quotient Identities

image.alt: Example combining reciprocal and quotient identities
image.caption: Figure 7.9.6: Combining reciprocal and quotient identities to simplify.

step_by_step.calculation: (sin θ · sec θ)/tan θ = (sin θ · 1/cos θ)/tan θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Combine reciprocal and quotient identities to simplify complex expressions.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.7: Summary of reciprocal and quotient identities.

sections.introduction: Pythagorean identities are the most important trigonometric identities—they come directly from the Pythagorean theorem and the unit circle. The fundamental identity sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 is used constantly in trigonometry and calculus. The other two Pythagorean identities (1 + tan² = sec², 1 + cot² = csc²) come from dividing the fundamental identity by cos² or sin². Mastering these identities is essential for all trig work.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.9.8: Introducing Pythagorean identities derived from the unit circle and Pythagorean theorem.

key_concept.definition:
Three Pythagorean Identities:

1. Fundamental Identity:
sin²θ + cos²θ = 1
(From unit circle: x² + y² = 1 where x = cos θ, y = sin θ)

2. Tangent-Secant Identity:
1 + tan²θ = sec²θ
(Divide fundamental by cos²θ)

3. Cotangent-Cosecant Identity:
1 + cot²θ = csc²θ
(Divide fundamental by sin²θ)

Alternative Forms:
sin²θ = 1 - cos²θ
cos²θ = 1 - sin²θ
tan²θ = sec²θ - 1
cot²θ = csc²θ - 1

When to Use:
- Simplify expressions with squares
- Convert between sin and cos
- Convert between tan and sec
- Convert between cot and csc
- Solve trig equations
- Verify other identities
key_concept.context: Pythagorean identities are fundamental—they're used constantly in trigonometry and calculus. The fundamental identity (sin² + cos² = 1) comes from the unit circle equation (x² + y² = 1). The other two come from dividing by cos² or sin². In calculus, you'll use these for trigonometric substitution in integrals, for simplifying derivatives, for solving trig equations. In simplifying expressions, these let you replace sin² with 1 - cos² (or vice versa). In solving equations, these let you write everything in terms of one function. Understanding where these come from (unit circle) is better than memorization—you can derive them. This section teaches Pythagorean identities—the most important trig identities.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.9: Diagram showing derivation of all three Pythagorean identities from the unit circle.

worked_examples.title: Using Fundamental Pythagorean Identity

image.caption: Figure 7.9.10: Using sin²θ + cos²θ = 1 to simplify expressions.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Fundamental Pythagorean identity: sin² + cos² = 1; use to simplify expressions.

worked_examples.title: Using Tangent-Secant Identity

image.caption: Figure 7.9.11: Using 1 + tan²θ = sec²θ to simplify expressions.

worked_examples.problem: If sec θ = 5/3 and θ is in QI, find tan θ.

step_by_step.explanation: θ is in QI, tan positive in QI.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Tangent-secant identity: 1 + tan² = sec²; use to find tan from sec.

worked_examples.title: Using Cotangent-Cosecant Identity

image.caption: Figure 7.9.12: Using 1 + cot²θ = csc²θ to simplify expressions.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Cotangent-cosecant identity: 1 + cot² = csc², so csc² - cot² = 1.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.13: Summary of Pythagorean identities and their uses.

sections.introduction: Even/odd identities tell us which trig functions are even (symmetric about y-axis: f(-x) = f(x)) and which are odd (symmetric about origin: f(-x) = -f(x)). Cofunction identities relate complementary angles (angles that add to 90°). These identities are useful for simplifying expressions with negative angles and for relating different trig functions.

introduction_image.alt: Introduction to even/odd and cofunction identities
introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.9.14: Introducing even/odd identities (symmetry) and cofunction identities (complementary angles).

key_concept.definition:
Even/Odd Identities:

Even Functions (symmetric about y-axis):
cos(-θ) = cos θ
sec(-θ) = sec θ

Odd Functions (symmetric about origin):
sin(-θ) = -sin θ
tan(-θ) = -tan θ
csc(-θ) = -csc θ
cot(-θ) = -cot θ

Cofunction Identities:
(For complementary angles: θ and 90° - θ)

sin(90° - θ) = cos θ
cos(90° - θ) = sin θ

tan(90° - θ) = cot θ
cot(90° - θ) = tan θ

sec(90° - θ) = csc θ
csc(90° - θ) = sec θ

Memory Tip:
Co-function means 'complementary function'
sin ↔ cos, tan ↔ cot, sec ↔ csc
key_concept.context: Even/odd identities come from symmetry—cos and sec are even (mirror image across y-axis), sin, tan, csc, cot are odd (rotate 180° around origin). Cofunction identities come from complementary angles in right triangles—if one acute angle is θ, the other is 90° - θ, and sin of one equals cos of the other. In calculus, even/odd identities help with integrals (integral of odd function over symmetric interval is 0). Cofunction identities help simplify expressions with complementary angles. Understanding these identities builds trigonometric fluency—you can simplify expressions with negative angles and complementary angles. This section teaches even/odd and cofunction identities—symmetry and complementary angle relationships.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.15: Diagram showing even/odd symmetry and cofunction relationships with complementary angles.

worked_examples.title: Using Even/Odd Identities

image.caption: Figure 7.9.16: Using even/odd identities to simplify expressions with negative angles.

step_by_step.calculation: sin(-θ) + cos(-θ) = cos θ - sin θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Even/odd identities: sin is odd (sin(-θ) = -sin θ), cos is even (cos(-θ) = cos θ).

worked_examples.title: Using Cofunction Identities

image.caption: Figure 7.9.17: Using cofunction identities to simplify expressions with complementary angles.

step_by_step.calculation: sin(90° - θ) + cos(90° - θ) = cos θ + sin θ
sin(90° - θ) + cos(90° - θ) = sin θ + cos θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Cofunction identities: sin(90°-θ) = cos θ, cos(90°-θ) = sin θ.

worked_examples.title: Combining Even/Odd and Cofunction Identities

image.alt: Example combining even/odd and cofunction identities
image.caption: Figure 7.9.18: Combining even/odd and cofunction identities to simplify.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Combine even/odd and cofunction identities to simplify complex expressions.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.19: Summary of even/odd and cofunction identities.

sections.introduction: One of the main uses of trigonometric identities is simplifying trigonometric expressions—making them simpler, shorter, or in a more useful form. This skill is essential for calculus (simplifying before differentiating or integrating), for solving equations (simplify first, then solve), and for verifying other identities. The key is knowing which identity to use when.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.9.20: Introducing how to use identities to simplify trigonometric expressions.

key_concept.definition:
Strategy for Simplifying:

1. Convert to sin and cos:
Replace tan, cot, sec, csc with sin and cos
Often simplifies the expression

2. Use Pythagorean Identities:
Replace sin² with 1 - cos² (or vice versa)
Replace tan² with sec² - 1 (or vice versa)
Replace cot² with csc² - 1 (or vice versa)

3. Factor:
Factor out common factors
Factor differences of squares
Factor trinomials

4. Combine Fractions:
Find common denominator
Combine into single fraction
Simplify numerator

5. Cancel Common Factors:
Cancel common factors in numerator and denominator
Simplify to simplest form

Goal:
Simplest form (fewest terms, no fractions if possible)
key_concept.context: Simplifying expressions is a core skill in trigonometry—you'll use it in calculus (simplify before differentiating/integrating), in solving equations (simplify first), and in verifying identities. The strategy is systematic—convert to sin/cos, use Pythagorean identities, factor, combine fractions, cancel. The goal is simplest form—fewest terms, no fractions if possible. In calculus, simplified expressions are easier to differentiate and integrate. In solving equations, simplified expressions are easier to solve. Understanding simplification builds algebraic fluency with trig functions. This section teaches simplification strategies—how to use identities to simplify trig expressions.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.21: Diagram showing strategy for simplifying trigonometric expressions using identities.

worked_examples.title: Simplifying Using Pythagorean Identity

image.caption: Figure 7.9.22: Using Pythagorean identities to simplify trigonometric expressions.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Pythagorean identity: 1 - sin² = cos²; use to simplify expressions.

worked_examples.title: Simplifying by Converting to sin and cos

image.alt: Example simplifying by converting to sin and cos
image.caption: Figure 7.9.23: Converting to sine and cosine to simplify expressions.

step_by_step.calculation: tan θ · csc θ = (sin θ/cos θ) · (1/sin θ)
tan θ · csc θ = sec θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Convert to sin and cos, simplify, then convert back to simplest form.

worked_examples.title: Simplifying Complex Fraction

image.caption: Figure 7.9.24: Simplifying complex fractions using trigonometric identities.

step_by_step.calculation: (sin θ + cos θ)² = sin²θ + 2 sin θ cos θ + cos²θ
(sin θ + cos θ)² = 1 + 2 sin θ cos θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Expand, group for Pythagorean identity, simplify using identities.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.25: Summary of strategies for simplifying trigonometric expressions.

sections.introduction: Verifying trigonometric identities means proving that two trigonometric expressions are equal for all angles where both are defined. This is different from solving equations (finding specific angles)—verifying identities proves equality for ALL angles. The key strategy is to work with one side (usually the more complex side) and transform it into the other side using identities.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.9.26: Introducing how to verify trigonometric identities.

key_concept.definition:
Strategy for Verifying Identities:

1. Work with one side:
Start with more complex side
Transform it into other side
Don't work with both sides simultaneously

2. Use identities strategically:
Convert to sin and cos (often helps)
Use Pythagorean identities
Factor when possible
Combine fractions when needed

3. Common techniques:
- Multiply by conjugate
- Get common denominator
- Factor out common factors
- Use Pythagorean identities
- Simplify step by step

4. Goal:
Transform one side to match the other
Show they're equal for all angles

Important:
Never assume the identity is true
Work with one side only
Each step must be valid for all angles
key_concept.context: Verifying identities is a core skill in trigonometry—it's proof that two expressions are equal. This is different from solving equations (finding specific angles)—verifying proves equality for ALL angles. The strategy is systematic—work with one side, use identities to transform it into the other side. In calculus, you'll verify identities constantly—for derivatives, integrals, simplifications. Understanding verification builds proof skills—you learn to show why something is true, not just that it's true. This section teaches verification strategies—how to prove trigonometric identities.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.27: Diagram showing strategy for verifying trigonometric identities.

worked_examples.title: Verifying Simple Identity

image.caption: Figure 7.9.28: Verifying a simple trigonometric identity.

worked_examples.problem: Verify the identity: tan θ · cos θ = sin θ

step_by_step.explanation: Work with more complex side (left side).
step_by_step.calculation: Left side: tan θ · cos θ
Left side = sin θ = Right side
Identity verified: tan θ · cos θ = sin θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Verify identity: work with one side, use identities to transform to other side.

worked_examples.title: Verifying Identity Using Pythagorean Identity

image.alt: Example verifying identity using Pythagorean identity
image.caption: Figure 7.9.29: Verifying identity using Pythagorean identities.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Verify identity: use Pythagorean identity to transform one side to match the other.

worked_examples.title: Verifying Identity with Fractions

image.caption: Figure 7.9.30: Verifying identity involving fractions.

worked_examples.problem: Verify the identity: (1 + sin θ)/cos θ = sec θ + tan θ

step_by_step.calculation: Left side = sec θ + tan θ = Right side
Identity verified: (1 + sin θ)/cos θ = sec θ + tan θ

worked_examples.concept_applied: Verify identity with fractions: split fraction, use identities to simplify.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.31: Summary of strategies for verifying trigonometric identities.

sections.section_title: 7.9.6 Common Errors and How to Avoid Them
sections.introduction: Working with trigonometric identities introduces specific pitfalls related to identity misuse, algebraic errors, verification mistakes, and domain issues. Recognizing common mistakes helps students develop careful habits and produce accurate results. Understanding where errors typically occur enables self-correction and better problem-solving.

introduction_image.caption: Figure 7.9.32: Overview of common errors when working with trigonometric identities.

key_concept.definition:
Common Errors:

1. Identity Misuse:
- Using wrong identity
- Applying identity incorrectly
- Forgetting restrictions (where undefined)

2. Algebraic Errors:
- Wrong factoring
- Wrong fraction operations
- Sign errors
- Canceling incorrectly

3. Verification Errors:
- Working with both sides simultaneously
- Assuming identity is true
- Not showing all steps

4. Domain Errors:
- Not noting where functions are undefined
- Dividing by zero
- Not checking restrictions

5. Pythagorean Identity Errors:
- Wrong sign in identity
- Confusing which identity to use
- Forgetting the three Pythagorean identities

Error Prevention:
- Memorize all fundamental identities
- Work with one side when verifying
- Show all steps clearly
- Note where functions are undefined
- Check work by substituting values
- Practice regularly to build fluency
key_concept.context: Identity errors are common—using wrong identity, algebraic mistakes, verification mistakes. Working with both sides simultaneously is a common verification error—you should work with one side only. Domain errors (not noting where undefined) are important—identities are only valid where both sides are defined. Understanding these errors develops identity literacy—knowing which identity to use, how to verify correctly, where identities are valid. In calculus, these errors lead to wrong derivatives and integrals. This section teaches error awareness—critical for accurate identity work.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.33: Diagram showing common errors when working with trigonometric identities and how to avoid them.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Identity Misuse Error

image.caption: Figure 7.9.34: Correcting errors in using trigonometric identities.

worked_examples.problem: A student wrote sin²θ + cos²θ = 0. Identify and correct the error.

step_by_step.explanation: Comes from unit circle x² + y² = 1.

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: sin² + cos² = 0; Correct: sin² + cos² = 1

worked_examples.title: Correcting Verification Error

image.caption: Figure 7.9.35: Correcting errors in verifying identities.

worked_examples.problem: A student verified an identity by working with both sides simultaneously. Explain the error.

step_by_step.calculation: Student worked with both sides at same time

Work with one side only
Transform it into the other side

step_by_step.explanation: Working with both sides assumes identity is true.
step_by_step.calculation: Must prove identity, not assume it
Work with one side only when verifying identities

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: worked with both sides; Correct: work with one side only
worked_examples.concept_applied: Verification: work with one side, transform to match other side.

worked_examples.title: Correcting Domain Error

image.caption: Figure 7.9.36: Correcting errors with domain restrictions.

worked_examples.problem: A student simplified tan θ · cos θ = sin θ without noting restrictions. Identify the error.

step_by_step.calculation: Student didn't note where tan is undefined

step_by_step.explanation: tan θ undefined when cos θ = 0.

step_by_step.calculation: tan θ · cos θ = sin θ (cos θ ≠ 0)

worked_examples.final_answer: Error: didn't note restrictions; Correct: tan θ · cos θ = sin θ (cos θ ≠ 0)
worked_examples.concept_applied: Always note domain restrictions where functions are undefined.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.37: Summary of common errors when working with trigonometric identities.

sections.introduction: Apply all skills related to trigonometric identities—reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, even/odd, cofunction identities, simplifying expressions, and verifying identities—to demonstrate mastery. These problems integrate multiple concepts and prepare students for advanced trigonometry and calculus.

worked_examples.title: Comprehensive Practice Set

image.caption: Figure 7.9.38: Practice problems covering fundamental trigonometric identities.

worked_examples.problem:
Complete the following problems:

1) csc = 1/?
2) tan = sin/?
3) sin²θ + cos²θ = ?
4) 1 + tan²θ = ?
5) 1 + cot²θ = ?
6) sin(-θ) = ?
7) cos(-θ) = ?
8) sin(90° - θ) = ?
9) cos(90° - θ) = ?
10) Simplify: sin θ · csc θ
11) Simplify: tan θ · cos θ
12) Simplify: (1 - sin²θ)/cos θ
13) Verify: cos θ · sec θ = 1
14) Verify: sin²θ = 1 - cos²θ
15) Verify: (1 + sin θ)/cos θ = sec θ + tan θ
16) If sin θ = 3/5, find cos θ (QI)
17) If tan θ = 2, find sec²θ
18) Simplify: sin²θ - cos²θ + 1
19) Simplify: cot θ · sin θ
20) Why work with one side when verifying?

step_by_step.step: 15) Verify: (1 + sin θ)/cos θ = sec θ + tan θ
16) If sin θ = 3/5, find cos θ (QI)

step_by_step.calculation: = (cos θ/sin θ) · sin θ = cos θ

step_by_step.step: 20) Why work with one side when verifying
step_by_step.calculation: To prove identity, not assume it's true

worked_examples.final_answer: 1) sin; 2) cos; 3) 1; 4) sec²θ; 5) csc²θ; 6) -sin θ; 7) cos θ; 8) cos θ; 9) sin θ; 10) 1; 11) sin θ; 12) cos θ; 13) Verified; 14) Verified; 15) Verified; 16) 4/5; 17) 5; 18) 2sin²θ; 19) cos θ; 20) To prove, not assume
worked_examples.concept_applied: Comprehensive practice demonstrating mastery of fundamental trigonometric identities.

image.caption: Figure 7.9.39: Detailed solutions to all practice problems.

summary: Fundamental trig identities: Reciprocal (sin=1/csc, cos=1/sec, tan=1/cot), Quotient (tan=sin/cos, cot=cos/sin), Pythagorean (sin²+cos²=1, 1+tan²=sec², 1+cot²=csc²), Even/Odd (cos, sec even; sin, tan, csc, cot odd), Cofunction (sin(90°-θ)=cos θ, etc.). Use identities to simplify expressions (convert to sin/cos, use Pythagorean, factor, combine fractions) and verify identities (work with one side, transform to match other). Common errors: wrong identity, algebraic mistakes, working with both sides when verifying, not noting domain restrictions. Identities are essential for calculus (derivatives, integrals), solving equations, and simplifying expressions. Mastery of identities builds trigonometric fluency—essential for all advanced trig work.

review_questions_image.alt: Comprehensive summary: fundamental trigonometric identities, reciprocal, quotient, Pythagorean, even/odd, cofunction, simplifying, verifying
review_questions_image.caption: Figure 7.9.40: Complete reference for fundamental trigonometric identities showing all identity types, simplification strategies, and verification techniques.
